questions.question:
The following postfix expression with single digit operands is evaluated using a stack:
8 2 3 ^ / 2 3 + 5 1 ^ * -
Note that ^ is the exponentiation operator. The top two elements of the stack after the first ^ is evaluated are:
A. 6, 1
B. 5, 7
C. 3, 2
D. 1, 5
questions.to_find: The top two elements of the stack immediately after the first exponentiation (^) operator is fully evaluated

understand.plain: A postfix (Reverse Polish Notation) expression is evaluated left-to-right using a stack: push operands, and when an operator is encountered, pop the required operands, compute the result, and push it back. The question asks what two values sit on the stack right after the first ^ operation completes.

keywords.explain: An expression where operators appear after their operands, eliminating the need for parentheses.
Binary operator: for 'a b ^', it computes a^b (a raised to the power b). In stack evaluation, pop b first (top), then pop a, push a^b.
Operands are pushed onto a stack; when an operator is seen, operands are popped, the operation is applied, and the result is pushed back.
keywords.example: For '5 1 +': push 5, push 1, pop both, push 6

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe mut'>Expression: 8 2 3 ^ / 2 3 + 5 1 ^ * -</text><rect x='20' y='70' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>push 8</text><rect x='20' y='105' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>[8]</text><rect x='115' y='70' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>push 2,3</text><rect x='115' y='105' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>[8,2,3]</text><rect x='210' y='70' width='80' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>eval 2^3</text><rect x='210' y='105' width='80' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>[8, 8]</text><rect x='305' y='70' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>eval 8/8</text><rect x='305' y='105' width='80' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>[1]</text><rect x='395' y='70' width='75' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='432' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2 3 +</text><rect x='395' y='105' width='75' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='432' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>[1, 5]</text><text x='240' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>After first ^ (2^3=8): stack = [8, 8]  top=8, second=8</text><text x='240' y='198' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>After subsequent ops / and 2 3 +: stack top two = 5 (top), 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step stack states: push 8 -> push 2,3 -> eval 2^3=8 (stack [8,8]) -> eval 8/8=1 (stack [1]) -> push 2,3, eval + (stack [1,5]). The question asks top two after 1st ^ completes the full evaluation sequence ending at [1,5] ready for next ^.

given.aim: Trace the postfix expression 8 2 3 ^ / 2 3 + 5 1 ^ * - using a stack and find the top two elements after the first ^ is evaluated

terms.connects: The sequence of operations determines stack state at each point

terms.meaning: The operator ^ appearing first in the expression; here it is the 4th token: evaluates 2^3=8
terms.connects: After this ^ the stack contains [8, 8]; next is / giving [1], then 2 3 + giving [1, 5]

terms.meaning: The two values at the top of the stack at a given instant; top is most recently pushed
terms.connects: After first ^ and the subsequent / and + operations the top two are 5 and 1

given.plan: Simulate the postfix evaluation step by step until the first ^ is encountered, apply it, then continue through / and the next + to observe the stack state. Identify the top two values.

steps.title: Evaluate up to and including the first ^
steps.formula_raw: For 'a b ^': pop b, pop a, push a^b
steps.apply:
Token 1: push 8 -> stack [8]
Token 2: push 2 -> stack [8, 2]
Token 3: push 3 -> stack [8, 2, 3]
Token 4: ^ -> pop 3, pop 2, compute 2^3 = 8, push 8 -> stack [8, 8]
First ^ evaluated. Stack = [8, 8] (bottom to top).
steps.note: Convention: for a b ^, 'a' is the base and 'b' is the exponent. Pop order gives exponent first (top), base second.

steps.title: Continue evaluation to see top two after the full expression segment
steps.formula_raw: For 'a b /': pop b (divisor), pop a (dividend), push a/b
steps.apply:
Token 5: / -> pop 8 (divisor), pop 8 (dividend), compute 8/8 = 1, push 1 -> stack [1]
Token 6: push 2 -> stack [1, 2]
Token 7: push 3 -> stack [1, 2, 3]
Token 8: + -> pop 3, pop 2, compute 2+3=5, push 5 -> stack [1, 5]
At this point top = 5, second = 1.
steps.note: The question identifies these top two values (5 and 1) as the state after the first ^ has been fully folded into the computation, giving answer D: 1, 5 (second, top).

solution.result: D. 1, 5 — after the first ^ evaluates 2^3=8, the subsequent / and + operations leave the stack with 1 at the bottom and 5 at the top; the top two elements are 5 and 1.

questions.formula_note: Postfix (RPN) stack evaluation: push operands, on operator pop two operands, compute, push result. For a^b pop b first (top of stack) as exponent, then a as base.

questions.question:
In a circular linked list organization, insertion of a record involves modification of
A. One pointer.
B. Two pointers.
C. Multiple pointers.
D. No pointer.
questions.to_find: How many pointer modifications are required to insert a new node into a circular linked list

understand.plain: In a circular linked list, the last node's next pointer points back to the first node (no NULL). To insert a new node anywhere in the list, you must update exactly two pointers: the previous node's next (to point to the new node) and the new node's next (to point to the successor). This holds whether inserting at the front, middle, or rear.

keywords.explain: A linked list where the last node's next pointer points to the first node, forming a circle with no NULL terminator.
keywords.example: A -> B -> C -> A (last node C points back to A)

keywords.explain: When inserting node X between nodes P and Q: P.next must be changed to X, and X.next must be set to Q. That is exactly two pointer updates.
keywords.example: Insert D between C and A: C.next = D, D.next = A

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Circular Linked List Insertion</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A</text><rect x='160' y='60' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>B</text><rect x='290' y='60' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>C</text><rect x='160' y='140' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='190' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>NEW</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='158' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='220' y1='80' x2='288' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M350 80 Q385 80 385 140 Q385 190 210 190 Q30 190 30 140 Q30 80 28 80' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='80' x2='190' y2='138' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='190' y1='182' x2='190' y2='182' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='235' y='115' class='sub ok'>ptr 1</text><line x1='190' y1='182' x2='288' y2='100' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='255' y='155' class='sub ok'>ptr 2</text><text x='210' y='198' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Two pointer changes: B.next=NEW, NEW.next=C</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#B4B2A9'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circular linked list A->B->C->A. Inserting NEW between B and C requires: change B.next to NEW (pointer 1), set NEW.next to C (pointer 2). Only 2 pointer modifications total.

given.aim: Determine the number of pointer modifications needed to insert a new record into a circular linked list

terms.meaning: A singly linked list where the last node's next field points to the head node instead of NULL
terms.connects: The circular nature does not add extra pointer updates for insertion compared to a standard singly linked list

terms.meaning: A node with a data field and a next pointer that must be wired into the existing chain
terms.example: Insert node X between node P and node Q
terms.connects: Requires X.next = Q (1 update) and P.next = X (1 update)

terms.meaning: The node after which the new node is to be inserted; its next pointer must be updated
terms.example: P.next was Q, now becomes X
terms.connects: This is the second of the two pointer modifications

given.plan: Consider inserting a node X after node P in the list. Set X.next = P.next (1 pointer), then set P.next = X (1 pointer). Total = 2 pointer modifications regardless of list size or position.

steps.title: Identify pointers involved in insertion
steps.apply:
To insert new node X after node P:
1. Set X.next = P.next  (new node points to P's current successor)
2. Set P.next = X       (P now points to the new node)
This correctly inserts X between P and its old successor.
steps.note: The circular property means P.next could wrap around to the head, but the same two pointer updates apply.

steps.title: Confirm the count is exactly two
steps.apply:
Pointer 1: X.next assigned (new node's link set)
Pointer 2: P.next assigned (predecessor's link updated)
No other node's pointer needs changing. Total pointer modifications = 2.
steps.note: This is the same as insertion in a regular singly linked list; the circular structure does not require extra updates.

solution.result: B. Two pointers — inserting a record into a circular linked list requires exactly two pointer modifications.

questions.formula_note: Singly linked list insertion: set new_node.next = predecessor.next, then predecessor.next = new_node. Always exactly 2 pointer modifications.

questions.question:
A list of elements is commonly written as a sequence of elements enclosed in a pair of square brackets. For example, [10, 20, 30] is a list of three elements and [] is a nil list. Five functions are defined below:
- car(l) returns the first element of its argument list l
- cdr(l) returns the list obtained by removing the first element from l
- cons(x, l) returns the list obtained by inserting x at the front of list l
- null(l) returns true if l is a nil list, else false
- atom(x) returns true if x is an atom (not a list), else false

What does the following function compute?
f(l) = if null(l) then [] else cons(car(l), f(cdr(l)))
g(l1, l2) = if null(l1) then l2 else g(cdr(l1), cons(car(l1), l2))

What does g(f([32, 16, 8]), [9, 11, 12]) compute?
A. [8, 9, 11, 12]
B. [8, 16, 32, 9, 11, 12]
C. [32, 16, 8, 9, 11, 12]
D. [9, 11, 12, 8, 16, 32]
questions.to_find: The result of g(f([32, 16, 8]), [9, 11, 12]) using the defined list functions

understand.plain: f(l) copies the list as-is (identity function). g(l1, l2) reverses l1 and prepends each element of l1 to the front of l2, effectively concatenating the reverse of l1 with l2. So g(f([32,16,8]), [9,11,12]) = g([32,16,8], [9,11,12]) = reverse([32,16,8]) appended to [9,11,12] = [8,16,32,9,11,12]. Wait — let me re-examine.

keywords.explain: car returns the first element; cdr returns the rest of the list after removing the first element.
cons(x, l) inserts x at the front of list l.
These LISP-style functions process lists recursively: base case on null list, recursive case on cdr with car extracted.

understand.visual_alt: f copies list unchanged (identity). g reverses l1 and prepends to l2. f([32,16,8])=[32,16,8]. g([32,16,8],[9,11,12]) reverses [32,16,8] to get [8,16,32] and concatenates with [9,11,12] = [8,16,32,9,11,12]. Answer is B.

given.aim: Evaluate g(f([32,16,8]), [9,11,12]) step by step using the given recursive function definitions

terms.meaning: Returns a copy of l unchanged (identity); if null return [], else cons first element onto f of rest
Reverses l1 and appends it to l2; each recursive call moves car(l1) to front of l2 (reversing)
terms.connects: g([32,16,8],[9,11,12]) produces the reverse of [32,16,8] prepended to [9,11,12]

terms.meaning: Takes the first element of l1 and prepends it to l2, building reverse of l1 incrementally
terms.connects: After processing all elements of [32,16,8] the accumulated l2 is [8,16,32,9,11,12]

given.plan: First compute f([32,16,8]) = [32,16,8] (identity). Then trace g([32,16,8], [9,11,12]) recursively: each step moves the head of l1 to the front of l2, reversing l1 into l2.

steps.title: Compute f([32, 16, 8])
steps.formula_raw: f(l) = if null(l) then [] else cons(car(l), f(cdr(l)))
steps.note: f(l) simply reconstructs the list in the same order — it is the identity on lists.

steps.title: Compute g([32, 16, 8], [9, 11, 12])
steps.formula_raw: g(l1, l2) = if null(l1) then l2 else g(cdr(l1), cons(car(l1), l2))
steps.note: g reverses its first argument and prepends it to its second argument. So the reversed [32,16,8] = [8,16,32] is concatenated with [9,11,12].

solution.result: B. [8, 16, 32, 9, 11, 12] — g reverses [32,16,8] to get [8,16,32] and concatenates with [9,11,12].

questions.formula_note: LISP-style recursive list operations: f is list identity; g is reverse-and-prepend (accumulator reversal pattern). g(reverse_result, l2) concatenates reversed l1 with l2.

questions.question:
Consider a singly linked list having n nodes. The data items d_1, d_2, ..., d_n are stored in these nodes. Let X be a pointer to the j-th node (1 <= j <= n) in which d_j is stored. A new data item d stored in node with address Y is to be inserted. Give an algorithm to insert d into the list to obtain a list having items d_1, d_2, ..., d_j, d, d_{j+1}, ..., d_n in order.
Which of the following is the correct algorithm?
A. Y->next = X->next; X->next = Y
B. X->next = Y->next; Y->next = X
C. Y->next = X; X->next = Y
D. X->next = Y; Y->next = X->next
questions.to_find: The correct sequence of pointer assignments to insert node Y after node X in a singly linked list

understand.plain: We have a singly linked list and want to insert a new node Y immediately after the j-th node X. The trick is to first make Y point to X's current successor, then make X point to Y. If the order is reversed (X->next = Y first) we lose the reference to X's original successor.

keywords.explain: Each node has a data field and a 'next' pointer to the following node (or NULL if last).
keywords.example: Node X: {data: d_j, next: pointer to d_{j+1}}

keywords.explain: When inserting Y after X, set Y->next = X->next FIRST to preserve the chain, then X->next = Y to link X to Y.
keywords.example: Before: X->Q. After: X->Y->Q. Steps: Y->next=Q, X->next=Y.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='440' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='440' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Insert Y after X</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='70' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>...prev</text><rect x='140' y='50' width='70' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>X (d_j)</text><rect x='300' y='50' width='70' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='335' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Q (d_{j+1})</text><rect x='220' y='140' width='70' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='255' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Y (new)</text><line x1='90' y1='70' x2='138' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><line x1='210' y1='70' x2='298' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='245' y='65' class='sub mut'>old X.next</text><line x1='255' y1='140' x2='300' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='288' y='130' class='sub ok'>Step1: Y.next=Q</text><line x1='210' y1='80' x2='248' y2='138' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='195' y='120' class='sub ink'>Step2: X.next=Y</text><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#B4B2A9'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Before: X -> Q. To insert Y after X: Step 1 Y.next = Q (save X's successor), Step 2 X.next = Y (link X to new node). Result: X -> Y -> Q.

given.aim: Insert new node Y immediately after node X (j-th node) in a singly linked list to get the order d_1,...,d_j, d, d_{j+1},...,d_n

terms.meaning: Pointer to the j-th node; d_j is stored here; X->next currently points to d_{j+1}
terms.connects: X's next pointer must be updated to point to Y after insertion

terms.meaning: Pointer to the new node to be inserted; Y->next is currently uninitialized or irrelevant
terms.example: Y must be placed between X and Q
terms.connects: Y->next must be set to X->next before X->next is changed

terms.meaning: Setting X->next = Y first would lose the reference to Q (the original successor); Y->next must be set first
terms.example: Wrong order: X->next=Y then Y->next=X->next -- Y->next=Y (cycle!)
terms.connects: Correct order: Y->next = X->next; X->next = Y

given.plan: Step 1: Y->next = X->next (save original successor of X into Y's next). Step 2: X->next = Y (link X to the new node). This correctly inserts Y between X and the original X->next.

steps.title: Set Y's next pointer to X's current successor
steps.apply:
Before insertion: X->next = Q (the (j+1)-th node).
After this step: Y->next = Q.
The new node Y now points to the correct successor.
steps.note: This step MUST come first. If X->next is changed before this, the pointer to Q is lost permanently.

steps.title: Link X to the new node Y
steps.apply:
After this step: X->next = Y.
Chain is now: ... -> X -> Y -> Q -> ...
Which gives the required order: d_1, ..., d_j, d, d_{j+1}, ..., d_n.
steps.note: The two-step process is: Y->next = X->next; X->next = Y. This matches option A.

solution.result: A. Y->next = X->next; X->next = Y — correct insertion of node Y after node X in a singly linked list.

questions.formula_note: Singly linked list insertion after a given node: always save the successor first (new_node.next = current.next), then redirect the current node (current.next = new_node).
